coredata.dc:title: Incorporating deep learning into smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on N, B-CDs for quantitative visual detection of silver ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xing, Haoming
Zhang, Yihao
Wang, Yu
Gao, Sineng
Liu, Xinru
Hu, Houwen
Chen, Jiale
He, Hongxing
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: With the acceleration of the modernization process, the heavy metal ion pollution caused by industry is increasingly serious, prompting people to take measures to maintain environmental health. Herein, we incorporate deep learning algorithm into smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (N, B-CDs) for quantitative visual analyzing silver ion (Ag+). Prepared N, B-CDs exhibit outstanding optical properties (537 nm, quantum yield: 38.7 %) and high sensitivity for Ag+ detection, which achieves a strong linear correlation of 0.039–70 µM Ag+ and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 39 nM. Particularly, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated with the deep learning algorithm facilitates a more intuitive and precise on-site determination of Ag+ through a dedicated website. Smartphone-captured images are applied to train a convolutional neural network (CNN), which predicts unknown Ag+ concentrations based on color feature convolution. With the blessing of CNN, designed website allows users to identify Ag+ from archived images of real samples, yielding the recoveries of 98.5 %–102.4 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 2.86 %. This work proves the feasibility of visualized detection based on smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated deep learning kernel and expands the application of multi-doping carbon dots in the field of environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen and boron co-doping
Silver ion detection
Smartphone-assisted sensing platform
Deep learning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials, instruments and experimental details of N, B-CDs
Construction and evaluation of smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated with CNN model
Detection of Ag+ and possible sensing mechanism
Smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated with deep learning algorithm for real-time detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, B-CDs (Inset: the HR-TEM image). (b) The size distribution of N, B-CDs. (c) XRD pattern of N, B-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of N, B-CDs. (e) XPS spectrum of N, B-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s (h) O 1s and (i) B 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL and PLE spectra of N, B-CDs (Inset: sunlight and fluorescence (under 365 nm UV) images of N, B-CDs solution). (b) PL spectra of the N, B-CDs excited in the range of 350–450 nm. (c) 3D fluorescence mapping for N, B-CDs. Normalized emission intensity of N, B-CDs under varying conditions: (d) UV light irradiation time, (e) storage time at room temperature and (f) NaCl concentration.

(a) With the increasing Ag+ concentration, variation in fluorescence spectra of N, B-CDs. (b) The scatter plot corresponding to the concentration of Ag+ and fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0). (c) A linear regression analysis correlating F/F0 with the concentration of Ag+ spanning 0.039 to 70 μM. (d) Lifetime decay curves of N, B-CDs and N, B-CDs with Ag+. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, B-CDs with and without Ag+. (f) FT-IR spectra of N, B-CDs and N, B-CDs with Ag+. (g) Schematic illustration of internal mechanism in sensing Ag+ based on N, B-CDs.

(a) Structure of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform and captured fluorescence images with different Ag+ concentrations. (b) Schematic diagram of the CNN model. (c) Convergence curve during the process of model training, validating and testing. (d) Final accuracy and cross-entropy loss of training, validation and testing sets with ten repetitions. (e) Confusion matrix diagram for CNN model. (f) A Linear relationship between predicted Ag+ concentration and actual Ag+ concentration. (g) The process to detect Ag+ concentration and test results for real samples by the website.
Schematic portrayal of (a) the synthesis approach of N, B-CDs and Ag+ sensing, (b) a smartphone-assisted sensing platform and (c) the application in real samples.
The comparison of sensing Ag+ in water samples for the website integrated with CNN model and HPLC.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The critical t-value at 95% confidence interval is 2.78.

head.ce:title: Incorporating deep learning into smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on N, B-CDs for quantitative visual detection of silver ion

School of Physical Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the acceleration of the modernization process, the heavy metal ion pollution caused by industry is increasingly serious, prompting people to take measures to maintain environmental health. Herein, we incorporate deep learning algorithm into smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (N, B-CDs) for quantitative visual analyzing silver ion (Ag+). Prepared N, B-CDs exhibit outstanding optical properties (537 nm, quantum yield: 38.7 %) and high sensitivity for Ag+ detection, which achieves a strong linear correlation of 0.039–70 µM Ag+ and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 39 nM. Particularly, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated with the deep learning algorithm facilitates a more intuitive and precise on-site determination of Ag+ through a dedicated website. Smartphone-captured images are applied to train a convolutional neural network (CNN), which predicts unknown Ag+ concentrations based on color feature convolution. With the blessing of CNN, designed website allows users to identify Ag+ from archived images of real samples, yielding the recoveries of 98.5 %–102.4 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 2.86 %. This work proves the feasibility of visualized detection based on smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated deep learning kernel and expands the application of multi-doping carbon dots in the field of environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the process of rapid industrialization, heavy metal ion pollution has become an important factor impacting people's living environment and health. Silver ion (Ag+), as one of the common heavy metal ions in our daily life, has been widely used in electricity, photography, jewelry manufacturing, catalyst and other fields [1,2]. Concomitantly, large amounts of industrial waste containing Ag+ are discharged into the environment [3]. Worryingly, excessive intake of Ag+ will result in serious damage to stomach, brain and immune system of human [4]. Meanwhile, Ag+ is also the familiar pollutants in medical examinations, which always exists in blood, urinary and central nervous systems [2,4,5]. The allowable maximum permissible content of Ag+ in drinking water is less than 930 nM by World Health Organization (WHO) [2]. Consequently, the development of a sensor to Ag+ is of paramount importance in the environmental monitoring. Until now, various methods including the electrochemistry, localized surface plasmonic resonance (LSPR) spectroscopy and capillary electrophoresis (CE), have been developed to detect Ag+ [5–7]. However, these methods are susceptible to the complicate pretreatment, complex operation process and tedious analysis time, which seriously impedes the development of the rapid visual detection field [8]. Hence, there is significant importance in devising a straightforward, swift and efficient method for detecting Ag+.

Currently, fluorescence analysis has demonstrated its effectiveness within the realm of environment sensing, which is attributed to various advantages including the low cost, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, rapid response and simple operation compared to other methods [9]. Carbon dots (CDs), a newly emerging zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon material, have garnered extensive attention owing to their exceptional physicochemical attributes, such as remarkable optical properties, low toxicity, favorable stability and the easily achievable preparation process [10,11]. At present, several types of CDs are available for Ag+ detection. For instance, Chen et al. created nitrogen-doped CDs for sensitive and selective Ag+ detection [12], while Qi et al. synthesized CDs co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen for sequential Ag+ detection [13]. However, these CDs have the disadvantages of short emission wavelength and low quantum yield (QY), constraining their detection sensitivity and impacting their practical application. Therefore, it is necessary to take measures to adjust the fluorescence properties of CDs. Owing to the comparable atomic radius between boron (B) and nitrogen (N) to that of C, being close neighbors in the periodic table, the properties of CDs can be effectively modified by doping B/N. Furthermore, the incorporation of B and N into CDs leads to the generation of additional holes and electrons within their structures, altering the chemical reactivity of CDs [14]. Although some works about the doping of N or B have been reported [15,16], there is still more space for improvement in these works.

Besides, the fluorescence detection method based on CDs can only detect Ag+ in laboratory environment with professional fluorescence analysis equipment, which is not suitable for the on-site, visible and quantitative detection. Compared with the traditional fluorescence method, the smartphone-assisted analytical device has garnered significant attention for its portability and simplicity [17]. Equipped with cameras, smartphones enable the capture of high-resolution images, which can be converted into red-green-blue (RGB) values using color recognition software. However, the spatial patchy of RGB values in fluorescence images greatly disrupts the accurate determination of analyte concentrations. Developing a method that automatically determines the precise concentration of analytes from fluorescence image identification is highly desirable. In fact, the progress in artificial intelligence (AI), with a notable contribution from deep learning, has dramatically simplified the analysis of complex image data [18]. It is highlighted that a convolutional neural network (CNN) represents a critical class of deep learning algorithms, extensively applied across various bio-analytical applications [19]. Furthermore, CNN requires less preprocessing compared to traditional chemometric methods and enables automatic feature extraction, such as achieving a 99 % determination coefficient in predicting amino acid mixture concentrations [20]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a smartphone-assisted sensing platform combined with deep learning algorithm for on-site visual detection of Ag+.

Herein, o-phenylenediamine and boric acid are applied in a hydrothermal method to prepare nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (N, B-CDs), which exhibits green (537 nm) emission with a QY of 38.7 %. Following the introduction of Ag+, the fluorescence of N, B-CDs quenches markedly and a linear correlation is established within the 0.039 to 70 µM range Ag+ (R2 = 0.998), achieving a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 39 nM. Whereafter, we incorporate deep learning algorithm into a smartphone-assisted sensing platform and create a website for convenient and timely Ag+ detection. Images are flagged by Ag+ concentrations to form training sources and developed CNN model convolutes color features of process photographs with predetermined concentrations. Meanwhile, established website integrated with the CNN assists users in estimating Ag+ concentration from archived real sample images, which yields the recovery rates between 98.5 % and 102.4 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 2.86 %. Therefore, our work will provide a universal strategy and associated functional designs for visually quantifying detection in environmental safety monitoring fields (see Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials, instruments and experimental details of N, B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Descriptions about the materials, instruments and experimental details were recorded in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and evaluation of smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated with CNN model
ce:section.ce:para: A portable and cost-effective smartphone device contained with test strips was designed using 3D-printing technology (Supporting Information). Meanwhile, based on the integrated development environment of PyCharm, we constructed a CNN image identification model and evaluated the performance through a self-built website.

The morphology and size distribution of N, B-CDs are obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, in which particles appear uniform and are distinctly isolated from one another (Fig. 1 (a)). Meanwhile, high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) reveals a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm in N, B-CDs, closely to the (100) plane of graphite (inset in Fig. 1(a)) [21]. Corresponding particle size distributes between 1 nm and 9 nm calculating an average particle size of 5.2 nm (Fig. 1(b)). From the Fig. 1(c), a broad diffraction peak at 21.8 ° in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is observed, attributed to the (002) facet of graphite. This observation further confirms graphitelike structures belong to N, B-CDs. In order to investigate the surface groups and compositions of N, B-CDs, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) spectroscopy are utilized. In Fig. 1(d), the FT-IR spectrum exhibits O–H/N–H, C=O, C=C and C–N stretching vibrations signals around 3172–3473 cm−1, 1643 cm−1, 1605 cm−1 and 1454 cm−1 [22]. Notably, distinct absorption peaks at 1250 cm−1 (C–B) and 1015 cm−1 (B–OH) confirm the successful doping of boron atoms into N, B-CDs. Elements composition and content of N, B-CDs are characterized by the full XPS spectrum, which shows major peaks at 192.70 eV (B 1s), 284.80 eV (C 1s), 399.30 eV (N 1s) and 532.90 eV (O 1s) with homologous contents of 15.72 %, 53.44 %, 9.09 % and 21.75 %, respectively (Fig. 1(e)). As depicted in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, three peaks at 284.49 eV, 285.37 eV and 288.83 eV are corresponded to C=C/C–C, C–N/C–O and C=N/C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1(f)) [23]. Meanwhile, pyridine N, amino N and graphitic N of N, B-CDs are identified in N 1s spectrum, which is attributed to three peaks at 399.29 eV, 400.91 eV and 401.92 eV (Fig. 1(g)). As shown in Fig. 1(h), explicit C=O, B–O and C–O signals (531.70 eV, 532.30 eV and 533.10 eV) are subdivided in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum [24]. More importantly, for the B 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(i)), three peaks are deconvoluted at 185.87 eV, 192.00 eV and 192.70 eV, which falls within the B–C, B–N and B–O bonds, respectively. Therefore, the prosperous incorporation of B and N elements into CDs is supported by the findings obtained from both FT-IR and XPS analyses.

To understand the optical properties of N, B-CDs, we deeply research the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption, photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) spectra (Fig. 2 (a)). As exhibited in orange line of the UV–vis absorption spectrum, two obvious absorption peaks (209 nm and 240 nm) are vested in the π–π* transition of C=C, while another weaker peak is ascribed to C–O/C=O bonds by the n–π* transition [25]. Moreover, a broad peak at around 410 nm is found, which is corresponding to the n–π* transition of N/B-containing functional groups. Under the optimal excitation wavelength of 405 nm, maximum emission at 537 nm of N, B-CDs can be represented. In addition, the excitation spectrum of N, B-CDs overlaps well with the absorption peak at around 410 nm, which indicates that the surface states contribute to the observed emission [26]. Accordingly, the solution of N, B-CDs exhibits nearly colorless under sunlight, while it displays green fluorescence at the excitation of 365 nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 2(a)). Meanwhile, the proper excitation-independent fluorescence behaviors ranging from 350 to 450 nm prove the N, B-CDs possess uniform surface states (Fig. 2(b)). In Fig. 2(c), the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence mapping of N, B-CDs more intuitively exhibits a strong green emission center at 537 nm. Moreover, we calculate the QY of N, B-CDs to be 38.7 %, comparable to other CDs documented in prior research (Table S1).

To assess the capacity of N, B-CDs for environmental monitoring application, the fluorescence stability is a first important indicator to be considered. Prepared N, B-CDs maintain stable fluorescence intensity following extended UV exposure (0–40 h) and prolonged storage at 20 °C (0–30 day) (Fig. 2(d–e)). Meanwhile, Fig. 2(f) shows the fluorescence intensity of N, B-CDs in the NaCl solution with 0–1 M concentration, which maintains a stable fluorescence signal. These findings suggest that N, B-CDs possess outstanding photostability and resistance to photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ and possible sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: We verify the selective capability and anti-interference of N, B-CDs for Ag+ and a series of experimental conditions are optimized to obtain the optimal sensing performance (Figs. S1–S3). Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of N, B-CDs are explored to evaluate the sensitivity of Ag+ detection. With the Ag+ concentration increase, the emission of N, B-CDs gradually decreases and reaches the minimum value at 200 μM of Ag+ solution (Fig. 3 (a)). Fig. 3(b) exhibits the quantitative analysis of the fluorescence intensity change between N, B-CDs and Ag+ concentration in the range of 0–200 μM. From these results, it can be found that the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 (F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of N, B-CDs with the existence and inexistence of Ag+, respectively) is linearly decreased with the increase of Ag+ concentration from 0.039 to 70 μM (Fig. 3(c)). The calculated LOD of N, B-CDs for Ag+ is 39 nM, which is well below the standard (930 nM) set by the WHO [2], surpassing previously reported results (Table S2). The calculated LOD value of N, B-CDs for Ag+ is 39 nM, surpassing previously reported results (Table S2). Additionally, Fig. S4 explores fluorescence images of N, B-CDs solution under increasing Ag+ concentrations under UV lamp. Notably, the corresponding image exhibits a continuous transformation from green to dark yellow with the increase of Ag+, which will be beneficial to realize the visual quantitative detection in real samples.

Common quenching mechanisms encompass fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), electron transfer and dynamic or static quenching effect (DQE or SQE) [27,28]. Fig. S5 indicates paltry overlap between the emission and excitation spectra of N, B-CDs and the UV–vis absorption of Ag+, thereby eliminating IFE or FRET as potential explanations for the observed fluorescence quenching of N, B-CDs by Ag+. Meanwhile, to differentiate between DQE and SQE, fluorescence lifetime analysis is widely employed. As illustrated in Fig. 3(d), the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N, B-CDs in the absence and presence of Ag+ are measured. Double exponential fitting is selected to quantify the decay traces of N, B-CDs without and with Ag+ according to Eq. (1) [29]:
(1)
I
t
=
A 1
e x p
- t / τ
1
+
A 2
e x p
- t / τ
2
where A1 and A2 represent weight coefficients to fluorescence originating in two specific recombination components, denoted as τ1 and τ2 , respectively. Thus, the average lifetime (τ ave) can be determined by Eq. (2):
(2)
τ
ave
=
A 1
τ
1
2
+
A 2
τ
2
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The calculated average lifetimes of N, B-CDs without and with Ag+ are almost constant (3.31 ns and 3.29 ns), which suggests that the quenching mechanism of the detection of N, B-CDs toward Ag+ is SQE [27]. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S6, the Stern-Volmer plot presents a strong linear relationship between the ratio of emission intensity and Ag+ concentrations (0–70 μM), as demonstrated by Eq. (3) [30]:
(3)
F 0
/ F - 1 =
K q
τ 0
Q
=
K
sv
Q
In the equation, F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity without and with Ag+, respectively. Kq signifies the quenching rate constant, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, τ0 represents the fluorescence lifetime of N, B-CDs and [Q] is the concentration of the Ag+. As a result, the calculated Ksv is 0.02063 μM−1 and Kq is 6.23 × 1012 M−1·s−1. Generally, the Kq is greater than 1.0 × 1010 M−1·s−1 in SQE, further demonstrating that the quenching mechanism is SQE [31].

In addition, we further explore the UV–vis absorbance and FT-IR spectra to fully study the quenching mechanism. As the UV–vis absorbance spectra measured in Fig. 3(e), in comparison to the curve of N, B-CDs, the N, B-CDs with Ag+ solution exhibits obvious an attenuated signal with blue-shift from 289 to 268 nm and an enhanced weak at 435 nm, correlated with the creation of Ag–O and Ag–N bonds due to the reduction of C–O/C=O and C–N/C–B structure transitions [32,33]. The alterations in absorption bands are attributed to the complex formation between Ag+ and the O/N atoms within N, B-CDs. This direct coordination may cause the charge transfer between N, B-CDs and Ag+, ultimately causing the fluorescence quenching of N, B-CDs [34]. In the FT-IR spectra of N, B-CDs and N, B-CDs + Ag+ (Fig. 3(f)), a considerable blue shift and reduction can be seen in the vibration peaks of –OH/–NH, C=O, C–N and B–OH bonds. Notably, emerging stretching bands of 600–751 cm−1 and is affiliated with the Ag-O bond, which indicates the strong chelation between N, B-CDs and Ag+ to form complexes [35]. Furthermore, the zeta potentials of N, B-CDs exhibit a gradual decrease from −35.4 to +4.5 mV with the rising concentration of Ag+, which proves the strong electron-transfer process between N, B-CDs and Ag+ (Fig. S7) [36]. Therefore, as exhibited in Fig. 3(g), possible Ag+ sensing mechanism credited to the synergetic effect of SQE and electron-transfer is summarized in the schematic diagram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated with deep learning algorithm for real-time detection
ce:section.ce:para: Following the visualization of N, B-CDs fluorescence color changes, a series of fluorescence test strips with N, B-CDs are fabricated (Fig. S8). Nevertheless, the conventional quantitative detection method based on the test strips requires tedious steps to achieve image acquisition and is defective in color analysis precision [29]. Consequently, it is essential to realize the portable, visual, on-site and accurate detection by directly identifying the fluorescence images. Meanwhile, the CNN is a typical deep learning system originated from the human visual system to realize quantitative analysis of samples. Based on the CNN model, a sensing platform with test strips is 3D-printed assisted with smartphone to extract image data (Fig. 4 (a)), which is further analyzed in a CNN constructed by Python (the code of CNN is detailed in Table S3). Fluorescence images captured by an iPhone 15 smartphone exhibit an obvious color change from green to dark brown with the rising of Ag+ concentration from 0 to 200 μΜ. As displayed in Fig. 4(b), 2496 images attached to a series of Ag+ concentrations (six samples every concentration) are unified into 224 × 224 fixed-size as a dataset of CNN model. Of these images, 1872 flagged by known concentrations are extracted RGB triple channels into the input layer for training. In the process of training, fluorescence images undergo sixteen convolution layers, five mean-pooling layers and three full connection layers, finally divided into the concentration of Ag+ as output (Video S1). Remaining 312 images unflagged are utilized to assess the trained CNN model to evaluate the accuracy (Fig. S9). Fig. S10 presents the training history of the CNN model at 5000 epochs and the best result is considered according to reported criteria (the lowest value of the validation loss) [37]. Accordingly, Fig. 4(c) represents that the optimal result is found at 1927 epochs for developed CNN model and Table S4 exhibits the overall performance report for predicting Ag+ concentration. Moreover, the virtually unanimous results from ten repeated runs also demonstrate the reliability of the model and all prediction results are re-evaluated to generate a confusion matrix (Fig. 4(d–e)). The diagonal line of the matrix displays the proportion of accurate prediction samples, which predominantly clusters around the diagonal lines, providing additional evidence of precise prediction up to 99 % accuracy. The linear relation between predicted and actual Ag+ concentration is established and corresponding LOD as low as 65 nM (Fig. 4(f)). As illustrated in Fig. 4(g), a personalized website integrated with the CNN model is designed and parameters of the system get updated to accommodate real samples. Upon accessing the website, users choose real sample categories, upload images and then the site would predict and output the Ag+ concentration (Video S2). To appraise the feasibility of developed sensing strategy, tap water, wastewater and Yongjiang River water are applied in recovery experiments, which is compared to high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis. As shown in Table 1 , both methods exhibit Ag+ recoveries in real samples ranging from 98.5 % to 102.4 %, and the percentage of relative standard deviation (%RSD) <2.86 %. Meanwhile, calculated maximum t-test value of 0.68 falls below the critical value for a 95 % confidence interval, indicating that the two methods have no significant differences [38]. These results affirm the practicality and reliability of sensing Ag+ in real samples based on the smartphone-assisted sensing platform incorporated with CNN algorithm.

In summary, N, B-CDs with high green emission quantum yield (38.7 %) is hydrothermally synthesized, which possess excellent fluorescence properties and stability. Significantly, the N, B-CDs exhibit sensitive quenching response to different Ag+ concentrations from 0-200 μM, representing an excellent linear detection range from 0.039-70 µM (R2 = 0.998) with the LOD of 39 nM. In addition, we develop a CNN model to convolve the images color features, predicted Ag+ concentration as output with approximately 99 % accuracy and a LOD of 65 nM. The model is further incorporated with a website, allowing for the rapid, accurate, portable, and universal identification of Ag+ from real sample photos. Despite its merits, our sensing approach faces limitations regarding the deep learning algorithm and the complexity of real-sample assays. Hence, future work should focus on developing a more efficient and highly integrated detection system.

The author agrees to comply with the publishing ethics as described by journal and shall abide by the rules and regulations. The manuscript has been accepted by all the authors for publishing and all the authors has contributed to the work significantly as well.

Haoming Xing: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Yihao Zhang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software. Yu Wang: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Sineng Gao: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision. Xinru Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Houwen Hu: Visualization, Investigation. Jiale Chen: Software, Validation. Hongxing He: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis. Da Chen: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data (Figures S1–S10 and Videos S1–S2) to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112782.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
Figgures S1–S10.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile “off-on” pattern based on one-pot synthesis of N-doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of Ag+ and S2O3 2-
New J. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High quantum yield nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots for sensing Ag+, biological imaging and fluorescent inks
Anal. Methods
